summary: Joe Reis offers practical advice on asking good questions at conferences, emphasizing that poorly crafted questions waste everyone's time and can damage your professional reputation. He provides specific do's and don'ts, stressing brevity, relevance, and open-ended phrasing while warning against self-promotion, rambling, and contrarian showboating.
key_points: Good questions should be brief (one or two sentences) and open-ended
Bad questions are often self-serving and take away from the discussion's purpose
A reputation for asking bad questions becomes a lasting professional stain
Never use Q&A time to promote your company or show off by trying to school the speaker
Spontaneity in Q&A is valuable, but asking good questions still requires thoughtful preparation
money_quotes: Bad questions are often self-serving and selfish, and they take away from purpose of the discussion.
A reputation for asking bad questions is a public stain. Avoid it at all costs.
Don't try to show off by 'schooling' the speaker or panel. Don't be contrarian just to look cool in front of your peers. This is a no-win situation for you. The speaker has the stage. You don't.
Promote your company, product, or service. Nobody - I repeat, nobody - cares.
If you do this to me, I will mercilessly mock you until you cry or leave.
key_insight: Asking questions at conferences is a skill that requires self-awareness and discipline—your question should serve the dialogue and audience, not your ego or sales pitch.
